goal: Game Phase 2, Project 2 — Programmatic Pixel Art Sprites

risks: Per-zoom-level sprite caching multiplies offscreen canvas memory with no proven need — Canvas 2D drawImage scales efficiently at runtime; caching at N discrete zoom levels is premature optimization that adds complexity before a frame-drop is observed.

16x24 pixel arrays with H/K/S/P palette keys for 6+ agents is substantial upfront design surface. The palette abstraction layer (hair, skin, shirt, pants as separate keys) adds indirection before value is proven — misdesigning it means refactoring all sprite definitions later.

Furniture scope is broad (8 distinct types: desks, chairs, monitors, whiteboards, mailbox, bell, plants) at pixel scales where detail collapses. Getting 8 types visually readable at 16-32px could easily consume 2-3x estimated build time through iteration.
over_engineering_flags: Per-zoom caching: simplify to per-entity-type offscreen canvas at 1x scale only; add zoom-level variants only if benchmarking reveals actual frame drops.
Palette key system (H/K/S/P): ship 6 hardcoded per-agent sprite variants first; extract the palette abstraction layer only when agent appearance becomes configurable at runtime.
challenges.recommendation: Proceed with simplification — use hardcoded per-agent palettes instead of the H/K/S/P key system; cache at 1x only; combine furniture and floor/wall textures into a single environment-sprites initiative to prevent 3-way merge conflicts on renderer.ts.

initiatives.title: Programmatic Character Sprites
initiatives.user_scenario: The CEO opens the office and sees recognizable pixel-art figures at each desk — Alex in blue, Sarah in purple, Morgan in green, Marcus in orange, Priya in pink — instead of single-letter placeholders.
initiatives.scope: Audit the existing TILE_RENDERERS dispatch table, agentNameAt lookup, and current ctx.fillText character rendering in engine/renderer.ts. Define 16x24 pixel-array templates for all 6 named agents (Alex, Sarah, Morgan, Marcus, Priya, plus a generic fallback) using hardcoded per-agent color schemes — no H/K/S/P abstraction layer yet. Integrate sprite rendering into TILE_RENDERERS so all character drawing goes through the dispatch table. Add per-entity-type offscreen canvas caching at 1x scale so sprites are pre-rendered and reused per frame. No animation frames.
definition_of_done: All 6 named agents render as 16x24 pixel-art sprites — no letter placeholders (A, S, M, etc.) remain in the office view
Each agent has a visually distinct color scheme: Alex=blue, Sarah=purple, Morgan=green, Marcus=orange, Priya=pink, generic fallback=gray
Sprite rendering is wired into the TILE_RENDERERS dispatch table — zero ad-hoc rendering bypasses the dispatch pattern
Per-entity-type offscreen canvas cache is implemented — each sprite variant is pre-rendered once and reused each frame
No external asset files (PNG, SVG) exist — all sprite data is defined in TypeScript as pixel arrays
Zoom, pan, click, and hover functionality from Project 1 remain fully functional
npx tsc --noEmit && npx vite build complete with zero errors

initiatives.title: Furniture Sprites and Tile Textures
initiatives.user_scenario: The CEO sees a visually rich office with textured floors, subtle walls, and recognizable furniture shapes — desks, chairs, monitors, a whiteboard, mailbox, bell, and plants — replacing the flat color fills from Project 1.
initiatives.scope: Using audit findings from the character-sprites initiative (no separate codebase scan needed), replace flat color fills for all furniture tile types with procedural Canvas path drawings: desk (surface + legs), chair (seat + back), monitor (screen + stand), whiteboard (frame + writing surface), mailbox (box + slot), bell (dome), plant (pot + foliage). Replace flat floor and wall tiles with subtle texture passes — a light grid or grout pattern for floors, a baseboard stripe for walls. All renderers integrate with the existing TILE_RENDERERS dispatch table. No external assets, no animation.
definition_of_done: All 7 furniture tile types render with recognizable procedural shapes using Canvas paths: desk, chair, monitor, whiteboard, mailbox, bell, plant
Floor tiles display a subtle texture (grid lines, grout seams, or equivalent) — no flat solid fills
Wall tiles display a subtle texture (baseboard stripe, panel lines, or equivalent) — no flat solid fills
All furniture and tile renderers are wired into TILE_RENDERERS dispatch table — zero rendering bypasses the dispatch pattern
No external asset files (PNG, SVG) exist — all drawing is programmatic Canvas 2D paths
Zoom, pan, click, and hover functionality from Project 1 remain fully functional
npx tsc --noEmit && npx vite build complete with zero errors
